chunk_summary: Guidelines for feeding preterm and low birth weight infants, including feed advancement, human milk fortification, discharge planning, and growth monitoring using corrected age.
learning_objectives: Understand the stepwise advancement of enteral feeds in preterm infants.
Learn the role of human milk and fortification in meeting nutritional needs of very preterm infants.
Develop discharge plans including feeding schedules, supplementation, and growth monitoring.
Recognize the importance of corrected age in introducing complementary foods.
key_facts: Minimal enteral feeds should start within 24–48 hours in stable preterm infants, advancing by 10–30 mL/kg/day.
Mother’s own milk is the first-line choice; donor human milk is preferred over formula for very low birth weight (VLBW) infants.
Human milk fortification is recommended once enteral volume reaches ~100 mL/kg/day to support growth and bone mineralization.
Feeding transitions from orogastric/NG feeds to oral feeds as suck–swallow–breathe coordination develops (32–34 weeks gestation).
Discharge plans should include feeding schedules, fortified milk or post-discharge formula, and supplementation with vitamin D and iron.
Complementary foods should be introduced around 6 months corrected age when developmental readiness is present.
reference_quote: Mother’s own milk is first-line because it reduces necrotizing enterocolitis risk and is better tolerated; if unavailable, pasteurized donor human milk is preferred over formula for VLBW infants. (Source: WHO, Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Human milk fortification for preterm infants
